caveats: a: Occupation evidence is for a broader chemical-manufacturing group, not NAICS 325311 alone.
a: The injected compensation ratio is unusually low and reinforces capital intensity, but it is frozen dataset input rather than evidence for task exposure.
rho: No industry adoption study directly measures the five-year implementable share.
rho: Implementation in back-office workflows should be faster than in process control, creating substantial plant-to-plant dispersion.
e: Public industry data do not measure the contract-manufacturing share.
e: USGS reports only 18 ammonia-producing companies in 2025, suggesting concentration at the upstream end, while the NAICS definition also covers smaller downstream and waste-based operations.
s5: Owner age is neither industry-specific nor a direct observation of sales.
s5: The qualifying lens likely has a different ownership profile from large ammonia producers documented by USGS.
q: No public contract sample was available.
q: Capture differs materially between commodity ammonia and differentiated waste-derived or customer-specific products.
d5: Consumption data combine nitrogen, phosphate, and potash, though the source separately identifies nitrogen's share.
d5: Ammonia production includes industrial uses and does not isolate qualifying LMM contract manufacturers.
o: Operator-required demand can consolidate into larger integrated plants or imports even when the accountable production role persists.
o: Labor automation is accounted for in a and rho, not deducted again here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring industrial or agricultural supply programs priced per ton, batch, or contracted capacity, usually with natural-gas, ammonia, freight, or nutrient-market repricing and customer specifications for nutrient content and physical form.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS definition combines large commodity nitrogen production, fertilizer made from waste, and in-plant mixing. The lens is narrowed to repeat toll, private-label, waste-conversion, and customer-specific manufacturing so it represents an outsourced service rather than a commodity position.
lens.excluded_activities: Large integrated commodity ammonia and urea plants selling standard output, captive production, distributors and applicators, mixing-only firms classified in 325314, compost producers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly manufacturing or formulating nitrogenous fertilizer for external customers under toll, private-label, waste-conversion, or customer-specific supply programs.

a.bridge: BLS reports that chemical equipment operators, first-line production supervisors, chemical plant operators, industrial machinery mechanics, and related physical roles are the largest occupations across chemical manufacturing groups including 3253. I treat planning, quality and environmental records, procurement, maintenance knowledge, inventory, customer documentation, and exception analysis as AI-exposed, but not continuous process operation, sampling, material movement, repairs, or process-safety accountability. The broad chemical occupation mix may overstate transferable office-task exposure in highly capital-intensive nitrogen plants.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for a broader chemical-manufacturing group, not NAICS 325311 alone.
The injected compensation ratio is unusually low and reinforces capital intensity, but it is frozen dataset input rather than evidence for task exposure.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task and time studies from independent nitrogenous-fertilizer plants, including current DCS and laboratory automation, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Ammonia and downstream nitrogen production are continuous, physical, and safety-critical. AI exposure is concentrated in documentation, planning, quality, maintenance coordination, energy analysis, compliance, and commercial support rather than direct equipment operation.

d5.bridge: USDA measures total US fertilizer consumption rather than five-year constant-quality demand for outsourced nitrogen manufacturing. It reports fertilizer consumption stabilized after 2014 through 2020, fell 9.4% in 2021, and that nitrogen averaged 59% of fertilizer volume during 2006-23. USGS estimates US ammonia production rose from 13.6 million metric tons nitrogen content in 2024 to 14.0 million in 2025 and states nitrogen is an essential plant nutrient with no substitute. I use these as a flat-demand anchor; aggregate nutrient use may overstate demand for the narrow domestic outsourced subset when imports or integrated suppliers gain share.
caveats: Consumption data combine nitrogen, phosphate, and potash, though the source separately identifies nitrogen's share.
Ammonia production includes industrial uses and does not isolate qualifying LMM contract manufacturers.
d5.change_evidence: USDA baseline crop acreage and application-rate projections, ARMS nitrogen-use data, US apparent consumption including trade, and eligible firms' contracted backlogs would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Annual crop nutrient requirements support durable volume, while better nutrient-use efficiency, crop acreage changes, imports, and volatile farm economics limit growth. A flat year-five constant-quality quantity is the balanced base case.

caveats: Public industry data do not measure the contract-manufacturing share.
USGS reports only 18 ammonia-producing companies in 2025, suggesting concentration at the upstream end, while the NAICS definition also covers smaller downstream and waste-based operations.
e.change_evidence: A roster of the injected LMM firms coded from customer contracts, private-label or tolling disclosures, beneficial ownership, plant scale, and interviews would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: Most nitrogenous-fertilizer value is large-scale commodity manufacturing. The eligible recurring outsourced subset is limited to toll, private-label, customer-specific, or waste-conversion operators that are independent, transferable, and within the LMM band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325311 ($1,046,442,115) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325311 ($18,737,202,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $2,713,963 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325311); class <5: 61 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,837,803 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $330,988 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 27 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,303,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $414,890 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,347,632 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,323,308 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,106,414 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,341,565 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $103,470,444 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,635,027 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $932,942,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $168,023,014 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 48

caveats: Operator-required demand can consolidate into larger integrated plants or imports even when the accountable production role persists.
Labor automation is accounted for in a and rho, not deducted again here.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers broadly insourcing production, agronomic substitution eliminating manufactured nitrogen, or certified autonomous plants operating without an accountable producer would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining nitrogen fertilizer quantity requires an accountable operator to convert atmospheric nitrogen and feedstock into ammonia or downstream fertilizer, manage hazardous high-pressure equipment, test nutrient content, and comply with emissions and effluent duties. Software does not self-serve physical nitrogen fertilizer.

caveats: No public contract sample was available.
Capture differs materially between commodity ammonia and differentiated waste-derived or customer-specific products.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract set showing open-book terms, natural-gas and ammonia escalators, renewal cadence, nutrient-price benchmarks, cost-down provisions, minimum volumes, and freight allocation would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Per-ton fertilizer pricing, global nutrient benchmarks, natural-gas and ammonia input sensitivity, transparent bids, and annual agricultural purchasing cycles pass through much of an implemented saving. Private-label specifications, waste feedstock access, local logistics, and reliable capacity allow partial retention.

caveats: No industry adoption study directly measures the five-year implementable share.
Implementation in back-office workflows should be faster than in process control, creating substantial plant-to-plant dispersion.
rho.change_evidence: Audited AI deployments in ammonia or fertilizer plants showing accepted outputs, removed labor hours, process deviations, override rates, and compliance outcomes would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Document, planning, laboratory-review, maintenance, and commercial copilots can be implemented within five years, but hazardous high-pressure processes, greenhouse-gas and wastewater reporting, cybersecurity, validation, and skilled-operator accountability slow deployment and require human review.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding US employer-business owners were at least 55 in 2018. I translate broad succession pressure into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability and reduce it for concentrated corporate ownership, family transfers, internal reorganizations, closures, environmental diligence, and strategic rather than LMM sales. The all-industry owner statistic likely overstates owner-retirement exposure in capital-intensive nitrogen production.
caveats: Owner age is neither industry-specific nor a direct observation of sales.
The qualifying lens likely has a different ownership profile from large ammonia producers documented by USGS.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, or GF Data deal histories for US nitrogen-fertilizer, waste-to-fertilizer, and private-label nutrient producers, paired with current ownership and owner-age interviews, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: Eligible smaller formulation and waste-conversion firms can face succession, but major ammonia assets are generally corporate and specialized liabilities can prevent a clean control transfer.

principal_driver: Essential recurring crop nutrient demand supports physical production and repeat customer programs.
principal_weakness: Commodity economics, very low labor intensity, and a scarce outsourced-service subset constrain both AI benefit and capture.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as nitrogenous fertilizer-material manufacture, fertilizer manufacture from sewage or animal waste, and nitrogenous materials mixed with other ingredients.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 325311 Nitrogenous Fertilizer Manufacturing

sources.supports: Lists chemical equipment operators, production supervisors, chemical plant operators, industrial machinery mechanics, and related plant roles among the largest occupations in chemical manufacturing groups including 3253.
sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024

sources.supports: Reports that production occupations comprised about half of manufacturing jobs in 2024 and connects automated-machinery adoption to continuing mechanic demand.
sources.title: Producing the goods of the future: Job opportunities in manufacturing

sources.supports: Describes ammonia manufacture from atmospheric nitrogen and hydrogen, downstream urea and ammonium products, and regulated wastewater streams including condensate, blowdown, spills, leaks, and runoff.
sources.title: Fertilizer Manufacturing Effluent Guidelines

sources.supports: Defines covered ammonia processes and specifies reporting of process CO2 and combustion CO2, methane, and nitrous oxide, along with calculation and record duties.
sources.title: Subpart G Information Sheet: Ammonia Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 ABS, data year 2018.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages: Over Half of U.S. Business Owners Were Age 55 and Over

sources.supports: Reports 18 companies at 38 US plants in 2025, about 80% capacity utilization, estimated production of 14.0 million metric tons versus 13.6 million in 2024, 88% of domestic ammonia production for fertilizer, and no substitute for nitrogen as a plant nutrient.
sources.title: Mineral Commodity Summaries 2026: Nitrogen (Fixed)—Ammonia

sources.supports: Reports declining US fertilizer production and consumption relative to 2006, nitrogen consumption above phosphate and potash combined, and strong roles for energy costs, crop prices, and global trade.
sources.title: Drivers of Fertilizer Markets: Supply, Demand, and Prices

sources.supports: Reports 21 million metric tons total fertilizer consumption in 2013, stabilization after 2014 through 2020, a 9.4% fall to 18.3 million in 2021, and nitrogen averaging 59% of volume during 2006-23.
sources.title: U.S. fertilizer consumption rebounds from 2021 drop

title: Nitrogenous Fertilizer Manufacturing
